<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Before outlining the backend plan, I need to ask the user about key architectural choices that would significantly change the approach.

The recommended architecture extracts a dedicated `SchaerbeekMunicipality.Api` project for the backend. It would contain handlers, validators, endpoints, and authentication logic. A few targeted questions may still be needed to finalize the approach.

Three implementation paths are being weighed. Blazor Server could act as a BFF, calling the API via HttpClient while forwarding officer context through headers. A full Blazor WASM migration would require client-side authentication. Alternatively, the projects could remain in the same process initially, separated only by clean boundaries, before introducing HTTP communication.

The pragmatic choice is Option A. Blazor Server will act as a BFF and call the separate API project via HttpClient. The recommended structure uses three layers: Application for handlers and DTOs, Api as the minimal endpoint host, and Web as the Blazor UI only. Shared contracts and DTOs would live in the Application layer.

A typed HttpClient client project would wrap API endpoints. Authentication should move from the demo ICurrentOfficer to JWT or cookie-based tokens. The plan still needs confirmation on the BFF versus WASM path and whether real authentication is required immediately.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Blazor Server will act as a backend-for-frontend. The UI will call a separate API via HttpClient.

The demo officer switcher will stay for now. Real authentication will come later.

The target architecture uses three projects under AppHost: Postgres, a minimal API host, and a pure Blazor Server web project.

Application logic will move into a new Application project. It will reference only the Domain layer and contain no ASP.NET dependencies. The API project will host the actual endpoints.

The API project will register handlers and map endpoints in Program.cs. It will include middleware to read X-Officer headers and set the current officer context.

A thin Contracts project may hold shared DTOs. The Web project will reference it or the Application layer for types only. Application handlers will remain out of the Web project. The Web project will instead consume the API through an ApiClient service.

The Web project will drop its handlers, endpoints, and Infrastructure dependency. It will inject an API client instead of individual handlers. A delegating handler will attach officer headers from the current context.

The migration proceeds in four phases. First extract the Application and API projects and wire Aspire. Then add API client abstractions. Then migrate features one by one. Finally remove all handler registrations from the Web project.

The API will host all existing endpoints from day one. The Web project will then switch pages one by one to the API client.

Integration tests currently run against the Web project via WebApplicationFactory. These must move to the API project. Web integration tests will become optional or end-to-end tests.

The demo authentication middleware will read the X-Demo-Officer-Id header from incoming requests.

Three API client patterns were compared. One client per feature area mirrors the existing handler pattern most closely. This makes migration straightforward.

Feature-scoped API clients will mirror the existing handler pattern. Each client maps to a specific endpoint route, keeping Blazor pages readable.

File uploads will move from direct handler calls to multipart form POSTs to dedicated document endpoints.

Error handling needs review. Handlers currently throw UnauthorizedAccessException and ValidationException. The API must map these consistently to ProblemDetails responses.

The concrete file migration is now defined. Handlers, requests, responses, validators, guards, and authorization classes will move into the Application project. Endpoints will move into the API project. The Web project will retain only its pages, components, and design system.

The Web project will reference only Contracts or Application for types, plus ServiceDefaults. It will drop the Infrastructure reference entirely. The API project will reference Application, Infrastructure, and ServiceDefaults.

The Aspire AppHost will wire the API and Web projects together. The Web project will reference the API for service discovery and expose external HTTP endpoints. The Web project will configure an HttpClient targeting the API base address. It will also register the officer forwarding handler to attach identity
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I have enough context to outline a full backend integration strategy.

The plan moves the application layer into a new `SchaerbeekMunicipality.Application` project and creates a dedicated `SchaerbeekMunicipality.Api` backend host. The existing `Web` project becomes a lightweight Blazor BFF using HTTP clients. AppHost will orchestrate the multi-project setup. Migration proceeds incrementally by feature area. Integration tests move to the Api project. Existing REST endpoints remain untouched. The Blazor EF concurrency issues disappear because each API request gets its own DbContext scope.

API clients should use three feature-ped interfaces instead of dozens of mirrored handlers. The migration follows a strangler fig pattern: set up infrastructure first, then move Registration end-to-end as the pilot, followed by BirthDeclaration and ChangeOfAddress. Only about eighteen Razor files inject handlers directly, so cleanup is manageable. A mermaid diagram will illustrate the architecture.
</details></think>

`Application/DependencyInjection.cs`:\n\n```csharp\npublic static IServiceCollection AddApplication(this IServiceCollection services)\n{\n    services.AddValidatorsFromAssemblyContaining\u003cOpenRegistrationCaseValidator\u003e();\n    services.AddScoped\u003cICurrentOfficer, CurrentOfficer\u003e();\n    // all ~60 handler + authorization registrations (moved from Web Program.cs)\n    return services;\n}\n```\n\n### 1.2 Add `SchaerbeekMunicipality.Api`\n\nNew ASP.NET Core host with:\n\n- `builder.AddServiceDefaults()`\n- `builder.Services.AddInfrastructure(...)` + `AddApplication()`\n- `await app.Services.InitializeDatabaseAsync()` (moved from Web)\n- Endpoint mapping: `MapRegistrationEndpoints()`, etc. (move `*Endpoint.cs` files here)\n- `MapOpenApi()`, health endpoints\n- **No** Razor / MudBlazor\n\nAdd global exception handling middleware mapping:\n\n| Exception | HTTP status |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| `FluentValidation.ValidationException` | 400 + validation problem |\n| `UnauthorizedAccessException` | 403 |\n| `KeyNotFoundException` | 404 |\n| Domain transition exceptions | 409 |\n\nEndpoints today return `ValidationResults.ToProblemDetails` for pre-handler validation; handlers still throw for auth/not-found. Central middleware keeps HTTP semantics consistent for the Blazor client.\n\n### 1.3 Demo officer forwarding (your chosen auth approach)\n\n**Api** — middleware reads headers and sets scoped `ICurrentOfficer`:\n\n```\nX-Demo-Officer-Id: {guid}\nX-Demo-Officer-Role: PopulationOfficer | ReceptionOfficer | PoliceClerk\nX-Demo-Officer-Name: {display name}\n```\n\nReject requests with missing/invalid headers in non-Development (optional tightening later).\n\n**Web** — `DelegatingHandler` attached to all API HttpClients:\n\n```csharp\nprotected override Task\u003cHttpResponseMessage\u003e SendAsync(HttpRequestMessage request, ...)\n{\n    request.Headers.Add(\"X-Demo-Officer-Id\", _officer.OfficerId.ToString());\n    request.Headers.Add(\"X-Demo-Officer-Role\", _officer.Role.ToString());\n    request.Headers.Add(\"X-Demo-Officer-Name\", _officer.DisplayName);\n    return base.SendAsync(request, cancellationToken);\n}\n```\n\n[`RoleSwitcher.razor`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Auth/Components/RoleSwitcher.razor) stays in Web unchanged; only the transport of identity changes.\n\n### 1.4 Update Aspire AppHost\n\n[`AppHost.cs`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.AppHost/AppHost.cs) becomes:\n\n```csharp\nvar postgres = builder.AddPostgres(\"postgres\").WithDataVolume().AddDatabase(\"schaerbeek\");\n\nvar api = builder.AddProject\u003cProjects.SchaerbeekMunicipality_Api\u003e(\"api\")\n    .WithReference(postgres)\n    .WaitFor(postgres);\n\nbuilder.AddProject\u003cProjects.SchaerbeekMunicipality_Web\u003e(\"web\")\n    .WithReference(api)   // Aspire service discovery\n    .WithExternalHttpEndpoints();\n```\n\nWeb registers HttpClients with base address `https+http://api` (Aspire service discovery URL).\n\n---\n\n## Phase 2 — Typed API clients in Web (BFF layer)\n\nCreate [`SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Api/`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/) with **3 feature-scoped clients** (not 60 individual ones):\n\n| Interface | Covers |\n|-----------|--------|\n| `IRegistrationApi` | All `/api/registration/*` operations |\n| `IBirthDeclarationApi` | All `/api/birth-declarations/*` |\n| `IChangeOfAddressApi` | All `/api/change-of-address/*` |\n\nEach method mirrors an existing endpoint contract, reusing Application DTOs (`OpenRegistrationCaseRequest`, `GetRegistrationCaseResponse`, etc.) so types stay identical.\n\nExample shape:\n\n```csharp\npublic interface IRegistrationApi\n{\n    Task\u003cGetRegistrationCaseResponse\u003e GetCaseAsync(Guid id, CancellationToken ct);\n    Task\u003cOpenRegistrationCaseResponse\u003e OpenCaseAsync(OpenRegistrationCaseRequest request, CancellationToken ct);\n    Task AttachDocumentAsync(Guid caseId, Stream file, string fileName, DocumentType type, CancellationToken ct);\n    // ...\n}\n```\n\nImplementation uses `HttpClient` + `System.Net.Http.Json` (`PostAsJsonAsync`, `GetFromJsonAsync`). For document upload/download, use `MultipartFormDataContent` and stream responses — matching existing [`AttachDocumentEndpoint`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/AttachDocument/AttachDocumentEndpoint.cs) behavior.\n\nAdd a small **`ApiException`** helper that reads `ValidationProblemDetails` from 400 responses and rethrows as `FluentValidation.ValidationException` so existing Blazor catch blocks (e.g. in [`ResidenceStep.razor`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/Components/ResidenceStep.razor)) keep working with minimal changes.\n\nRegister in Web `Program.cs`:\n\n```csharp\nbuilder.Services.AddTransient\u003cOfficerForwardingHandler\u003e();\n\nbuilder.Services.AddHttpClient\u003cIRegistrationApi, RegistrationApiClient\u003e(c =\u003e\n    c.BaseAddress = new Uri(\"https+http://api\"))\n    .AddHttpMessageHandler\u003cOfficerForwardingHandler\u003e();\n// same for BirthDeclaration + ChangeOfAddress\n```\n\n---\n\n## Phase 3 — Migrate Blazor pages (strangler pattern)\n\n**~18 Razor files** currently `@inject` handlers ([grep count](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web)). Migrate **one feature at a time**:\n\n1. **Registration** (largest, good pilot) — list pages, detail page, wizard steps, dialogs\n2. **BirthDeclaration**\n3. **ChangeOfAddress**\n\nPer-file change pattern:\n\n```diff\n- @inject GetRegistrationCaseHandler GetHandler\n+ @inject IRegistrationApi RegistrationApi\n\n- _case = await GetHandler.Handle(new GetRegistrationCaseRequest(CaseId), ct);\n+ _case = await RegistrationApi.GetCaseAsync(CaseId, ct);\n```\n\n**Blazor + EF concurrency rule becomes simpler:** the [`.cursor/rules/blazor-ef-core.mdc`](.cursor/rules/blazor-ef-core.mdc) guidance about shared `DbContext` per circuit largely goes away — each API call gets its own server-side scope. Pages can use `Task.WhenAll` for independent reads again (with normal HTTP caveats).\n\nWeb `Program.cs` after migration: remove all handler registrations, remove `AddInfrastructure`, remove endpoint mapping.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 4 — Tests and CI\n\n| Test project | Change |\n|--------------|--------|\n| [`Integration.Tests`](tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests) | Point `WebApplicationFactory` at `Api.Program` instead of `Web.Program`; tests stay HTTP-based and need minimal changes |\n| Domain.Tests | Unchanged |\n| *(optional later)* | Web UI smoke tests via Playwright against full Aspire stack |\n\nOfficer identity in integration tests: set demo headers on `HttpClient` default headers (same as production BFF forwarding).\n\n---\n\n## Phase 5 — Production concerns (defer real auth, but design for it)\n\nKeep demo headers for now, but structure so real auth is a swap:\n\n- Replace `DemoOfficerMiddleware` with JWT bearer validation\n- Replace `OfficerForwardingHandler` with token acquisition (cookie → bearer, or on-behalf-of)\n- `ICurrentOfficer` implementation reads from `ClaimsPrincipal` instead of headers\n\nDocument storage ([`LocalFileDocumentStorage`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Infrastructure/Storage/LocalFileDocumentStorage.cs)): stays on the **Api** host's filesystem initially. If Api scales horizontally later, swap `IDocumentStorage` for blob storage (S3/Azure Blob) without touching Application handlers.\n\n---\n\n## Recommended migration order (incremental, always deployable)\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart TD\n    P1[Phase 1: Application + Api projects exist alongside monolith]\n    P2[Phase 2: Api clients in Web]\n    P3a[Phase 3a: Migrate Registration UI to HTTP]\n    P3b[Phase 3b: Migrate BirthDeclaration UI]\n    P3c[Phase 3c: Migrate ChangeOfAddress UI]\n    P4[Phase 4: Remove handlers/endpoints/Infrastructure from Web]\n    P5[Phase 5: Real auth when ready]\n\n    P1 --\u003e P2\n    P2 --\u003e P3a\n    P3a --\u003e P3b\n    P3b --\u003e P3c\n    P3c --\u003e P4\n    P4 --\u003e P5\n```\n\nDuring Phase 1–2, both hosts can run: Api serves REST, Web still calls handlers in-process until Phase 3 starts — or jump straight to HTTP for Registration as the pilot.\n\n---\n\n## What stays the same\n\n- **Domain model and repository interfaces** — no changes\n- **Infrastructure / EF migrations** — no changes\n- **Vertical-slice folder structure** — preserved inside Application\n- **MudBlazor UI, DesignSystem, Municipal components** — stay in Web\n- **OpenAPI surface** — same routes, now owned by Api\n\n## Main risks and mitigations\n\n| Risk | Mitigation |\n|------|------------|\n| Large file move (handlers/endpoints) | Move whole feature folders in one PR per feature; git history follows |\n| Blazor error handling differences (exceptions → HTTP) | `ApiException` maps ProblemDetails back to familiar exception types |\n| Document upload/download over HTTP | Pilot with Registration attach/download endpoints first |\n| Latency vs in-process calls | Acceptable for municipal back-office; SignalR UI remains responsive with loading states already in place |\n| CORS | Not needed — Blazor Server BFF calls Api server-side (no browser cross-origin) |\n\n## Effort estimate\n\n- **Phase 1–2 (scaffolding):** ~1–2 days — mostly mechanical moves + DI wiring\n- **Phase 3 (UI migration):** ~2–4 days — 18 Razor files + dialogs, mostly find-replace patterns\n- **Phase 4 (cleanup + tests):** ~0.5–1 day\n\nTotal: **~4–7 days** for a clean split with demo auth preserved.\n",

_**User**_

Do you think this is a good move? Should just one folder API contains the whole backend a good idea or should we have also a kind of specific architecure for it ?

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Evaluating whether a single folder API should contain the entire backend or if a more specific architecture would be preferable.

Splitting into a separate API backend is a sound move for the right reasons, though not always necessary. Their existing Domain, Infrastructure, and vertical slice layers already provide strong separation. A single API folder may suffice for now, but a more specific architecture could help as the backend grows.

A single API project can still use layered architecture internally. The Application layer should remain the central boundary. Microservices are only needed if the backend grows too large. A monolithic design with clean layers is often better for a municipal application. The existing codebase structure supports this approach.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Good questions. Short answer: **yes, splitting is a sensible move for your situation**, but **one `Api` project is not the whole story** — the architecture that matters is the **layering around it**, not how many host projects you have.

## Is splitting a good move?

**For your codebase, yes — with the right motivation.**

You already have most of the hard parts:

- Clean **Domain** / **Infrastructure** split
- **Vertical slices** per use case (handler + request/response + validator + endpoint)
- **~70 REST endpoints** that integration tests already hit
- Blazor UI that **doesn't need** to live in the same process as business logic

So you're not doing a risky rewrite. You're **formalizing a boundary that already exists informally**.

### Good reasons to split (apply to you)

| Reason | Why it fits |
|--------|-------------|
| **Clear separation of concerns** | UI (Blazor) vs business/data (API) |
| **Independent deployment** | Scale or patch API without touching UI |
| **Multiple clients later** | Mobile app, partner integrations, batch jobs |
| **Real auth** | JWT/OIDC belongs on the API, not in Blazor demo switcher |
| **Fixes Blazor + EF pain** | No shared `DbContext` per circuit; each HTTP request gets its own scope |
| **Team scaling** | Backend and frontend teams can work in parallel |

### Reasons *not* to split (watch out)

| Pitfall | Your case |
|---------|-----------|
| **Premature microservices** | You don't need 5 APIs — one is fine |
| **Latency/complexity** | Blazor Server → HTTP → DB adds overhead vs in-process |
| **Distributed debugging** | Two logs, two processes, Aspire helps but it's more moving parts |
| **Over-abstraction** | Don't add MediatR, CQRS buses, etc. just because it's "real backend" |

**Verdict:** Splitting is a **good move** because you already have the API surface and clean domain layers. The cost is mostly mechanical (move code, wire HttpClients). The benefit is a production-shaped architecture without changing business logic.

---

## One `Api` folder vs a richer backend architecture?

**One `Api` host project is fine.** What matters is what's *inside* and *around* it.

### What I'd recommend (matches your plan)

```
Domain          ← pure business rules, no ASP.NET
Infrastructure  ← EF, storage, external integrations
Application     ← handlers, DTOs, validators, authorization  ← THIS is the real backend
Api             ← thin HTTP adapter only (endpoints, middleware, DI wiring)
Web             ← Blazor UI + HttpClients (BFF)
```

Here **`Api` is just the front door**. The backend lives in **Application + Domain + Infrastructure**.

That is already a **specific architecture** — clean/hexagonal with vertical slices — without splitting into many deployable services.

### What "one Api folder with everything" would look like (avoid)

```
Api/
  Controllers/
  Services/
  Repositories/   ← EF types leaking up
  Models/
  DTOs/
```

That's a **monolith inside one folder**: harder to test, harder to swap persistence, UI concerns mixed with business rules. Your current handler-per-use-case pattern is **better** than this.

### Do you need more than Application + Api?

For a municipal back-office app at your current scale: **no**.

| Pattern | When you'd add it |
|---------|-------------------|
| **Separate `Contracts` project** | Only if Web must not reference Application (e.g. NuGet-published client SDK). Optional. |
| **MediatR / command bus** | Only if handler count becomes painful to register manually. You have ~60 handlers — manual DI is still OK. |
| **Multiple API projects** | Separate bounded contexts with different scaling/security (e.g. public citizen portal vs internal staff API). Not needed yet. |
| **BFF per client** | One BFF (Blazor Server) calling one API is enough for now. |
| **Event-driven / message bus** | When outbound notifications become real async integrations (tax office, social security). Domain events in-process are fine for now. |

---

## Architecture diagram (what "good" looks like for you)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph presentation [Presentation]
        Web[Web - Blazor BFF]
    end

    subgraph apiLayer [Api - HTTP adapter]
        Endpoints[Minimal API endpoints]
        Middleware[Auth middleware ProblemDetails]
    end

    subgraph application [Application - core backend]
        Handlers[Handlers per use case]
        Validators[FluentValidation]
        AuthZ[Authorization and Guards]
    end

    subgraph core [Core and persistence]
        Domain[Domain]
        Infra[Infrastructure]
    end

    Web -->|HttpClient| Endpoints
    Endpoints --> Handlers
    Handlers --> AuthZ
    Handlers --> Domain
    Handlers --> Infra
    Infra --> Domain
```

**Api stays thin.** If endpoint files start containing business logic, that's a smell — logic belongs in Application handlers (where it already lives).

---

## Practical guidance

1. **Don't split for fashion.** Split because you want a real backend boundary, auth, and optional future clients. Your codebase is ready.

2. **Application is the backend.** Api is not "the whole backend" — it's the HTTP host. Name and structure it that way mentally.

3. **Keep vertical slices.** Moving `Features/Registration/OpenRegistrationCase/` as a unit into Application preserves cohesion better than `Controllers/` + `Services/` layers.

4. **One API project is enough** until you have a concrete reason for two (different security zones, different teams, different release cadence).

5. **Defer microservices.** Registration, BirthDeclaration, and ChangeOfAddress share people, households, documents — they're one bounded context today. One API reflects that.

6. **Real auth later is easy** if `ICurrentOfficer` stays the abstraction and Api middleware is the only place that populates it (demo headers now, JWT claims later).

---

## Bottom line

| Question | Answer |
|----------|--------|
| Is splitting a good move? | **Yes** — low risk for you, high clarity gain |
| Is one `Api` project enough? | **Yes**, as the HTTP host |
| Do you need more architecture? | **You already have it** — add **`Application`**, keep **Domain** and **Infrastructure**, keep handlers/endpoints as vertical slices |
| What to avoid? | Putting everything in Api, or jumping to microservices/MediatR without need |

The move isn't "create an Api folder and dump the backend there." It's **extract Application as the backend, Api as the adapter, Web as the client**. That's a deliberate architecture — just not an over-engineered one.

If you want to go deeper on one angle (e.g. Contracts project vs Web referencing Application, or when to split Registration into its own service), say which and we can walk through tradeoffs.

---

The user is asking whether Web will still reference Domain directly after the split, or if all communication must now flow through Application via HTTP or API calls.

Web would call Application APIs via HttpClient after migration. Application would host handlers, DTOs, and validators. Domain types are currently used directly by Blazor pages for display. The user's question is nuanced: whether Web will call Domain directly or if all communication must pass through Application.

Two separate concerns exist. Runtime business operations will flow Web → HTTP → Api → Application → Domain. For compile-time type references, Web may still import Domain enums or display types directly, or it may reference Application DTOs instead. The next step is to inspect Razor files to identify which Domain types Web currently consumes directly.

_**Agent (default)**_

You're mostly right — with one important distinction between **runtime behavior** and **compile-time references**.

## Runtime: yes, everything goes through Application

After the split, **all business operations** follow this path:

```
Web (Blazor)  →  HTTP  →  Api  →  Application handlers  →  Domain / Infrastructure
```

So Web will **no longer**:

- Inject `*Handler` classes
- Reference **Infrastructure** (no `DbContext`, repositories, `LocalFileDocumentStorage`)
- Load or mutate data in-process

Instead it calls typed clients like `IRegistrationApi`, which hit the API, which runs Application handlers. That's the boundary.

## Compile-time: Web may still *see* Domain types (lightly)

Web won't **call** Domain logic, but it may still **reference Domain types** for UI — and that already happens today.

Your DTOs embed Domain enums:

```36:39:src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/GetRegistrationCase/RegistrationCaseDetailDto.cs
public sealed record DocumentDto(
    Guid Id,
    DocumentType DocumentType,
    string FileName,
    DateTimeOffset UploadedAt);
```

And Razor uses Domain types for display:

- `AppStatusChip` → `RegistrationCaseStatus`
- `DocumentUpload.razor` → `DocumentType`
- `BelgianAddressFields.razor` → reference data types

Those DTOs move to **Application**, but they can still use `DocumentType`, `RegistrationCaseStatus`, etc. from Domain. So Web's references become:

| Project | Web references it? | Why |
|---------|-------------------|-----|
| **Application** | Yes | DTOs, request/response types, API client method signatures |
| **Domain** | Optionally (light) | Enums/value types exposed in DTOs and UI components |
| **Infrastructure** | **No** | Persistence stays on the API side |
| **Api** | **No** | Web talks over HTTP, not project reference |

## What "calling Domain" means vs "using Domain types"

| | Before | After |
|---|--------|-------|
| **Load a case** | `@inject GetRegistrationCaseHandler` → handler → repository | `RegistrationApi.GetCaseAsync()` → HTTP → handler → repository |
| **Show status chip** | Uses `RegistrationCaseStatus` enum from Domain | Same enum, from DTO returned by API |
| **Save to database** | Handler uses `IRegistrationCaseRepository` | Only Application/Infrastructure on API host |

Web never really called Domain aggregates directly — it went through handlers. That stays true; handlers just move behind HTTP.

## Strict vs pragmatic approach

**Pragmatic (recommended for you):**

```
Web  →  Application (DTOs + clients)  →  Domain (enums only, transitive)
```

Web references Application; Domain comes in transitively for enums in DTOs. No extra mapping layer.

**Stricter (optional later):**

```
Web  →  Contracts (UI view models, no Domain)  ←  Application maps Domain → Contracts
```

Useful if Web must be fully decoupled (e.g. separate repo, WASM, external team). For an internal Blazor BFF, it's usually unnecessary overhead.

## Summary

- **Behavior / data flow:** Web does not touch Domain or Infrastructure directly. Everything mutating or loading business data goes **Web → HTTP → Application**.
- **Types:** Web may still use Domain **enums and simple value types** indirectly through Application DTOs — that's normal, not a leak.
- **What Web must drop:** Handler injection, Infrastructure reference, any direct persistence access.

So: **Application is the backend contract for Web** — not Domain, and definitely not Infrastructure. Domain remains the core model that Application and Infrastructure use on the server side; Web only sees the slice of it that Application exposes in DTOs.

  "plan": "# Backend split roadmap\n\n## Why this roadmap exists\n\nThe current solution ([`SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web)) is a **monolith that already behaves like a split system**:\n\n- **Domain** and **Infrastructure** are cleanly separated\n- **~70 Minimal API endpoints** exist and are integration-tested via [`MunicipalAppFactory`](tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests/MunicipalAppFactory.cs)\n- Blazor pages **bypass HTTP** and inject handlers in-process (e.g. [`RegistrationCaseDetail.razor`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/Pages/RegistrationCaseDetail.razor) injects 13 handlers)\n\nThis roadmap formalizes boundaries that already exist informally. Web[\"Web — Blazor Server BFF\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph adapter [HTTP adapter — thin]\n        Api[\"Api — endpoints, middleware, DI wiring\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph backend [Backend — the real application layer]\n        Application[\"Application — handlers, DTOs, validators, auth/guards\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph core [Core and persistence]\n        Domain[Domain]\n        Infrastructure[Infrastructure]\n    end\n\n    Web --\u003e|\"HttpClient server-side\"| Api\n    Api --\u003e Application\n    Application --\u003e Domain\n    Application --\u003e Infrastructure\n    Infrastructure --\u003e Domain\n```\n\n| Layer | Role | Rule |\n|-------|------|------|\n| **Domain** | Entities, value objects, repository interfaces, policies | No ASP.NET dependencies |\n| **Infrastructure** | EF Core, repositories, file storage, domain-event side effects | Implements Domain interfaces only |\n| **Application** | Handlers, DTOs, validators, authorization/guards | **This is the backend.** No HTTP concerns. |\n| **Api** | Minimal API endpoints, middleware, OpenAPI, health | **Thin adapter** — no business logic |\n| **Web** | Blazor UI, MudBlazor, typed HTTP clients | **No Infrastructure.** No handler injection after migration. |\n\n**One `Api` project is enough.** Do not dump handlers, repositories, and EF into a single \"Api folder\" — that would be a monolith with a different name. The architecture lives in **Application + Domain + Infrastructure**; Api is just the front door.\n\n### Web, Application, and Domain — runtime vs compile-time\n\nThis was a key clarification from the design discussion:\n\n| | Runtime (what executes) | Compile-time (project references) |\n|---|------------------------|-------------------------------------|\n| **Web → business data** | Always **HTTP → Api → Application** | References **Application** (DTOs, client interfaces) |\n| **Web → Domain** | **Never** calls repositories or aggregates | May **transitively** see Domain enums in DTOs (`DocumentType`, `RegistrationCaseStatus`) — already true today in [`RegistrationCaseDetailDto.cs`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/GetRegistrationCase/RegistrationCaseDetailDto.cs) |\n| **Web → Infrastructure** | **Never** after migration | **No project reference** |\n\n**Application is Web's backend contract.** Domain remains the server-side core that Application and Infrastructure use; Web only sees what Application exposes in DTOs.\n\n### Auth strategy (phased)\n\n- **Phases 1–4:** Demo [`RoleSwitcher.razor`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Auth/Components/RoleSwitcher.razor) stays in Web; identity forwarded to Api via headers (`X-Demo-Officer-Id`, `X-Demo-Officer-Role`, `X-Demo-Officer-Name`); Api middleware populates `ICurrentOfficer`\n- **Phase 5:** Swap middleware/handler implementations — `ICurrentOfficer` abstraction stays; JWT/OIDC replaces demo headers\n\n### Vertical slices preserved\n\nKeep the existing per-use-case folder structure inside Application:\n\n```\nApplication/Features/Registration/OpenRegistrationCase/\n  OpenRegistrationCaseHandler.cs\n  OpenRegistrationCaseRequest.cs\n  OpenRegistrationCaseResponse.cs\n  OpenRegistrationCaseValidator.cs\n```\n\nDo **not** reorganize into `Controllers/` + `Services/` + `Repositories/` — that would fight the current codebase and add no value.\n\n---\n\n## Phase overview\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n    P0[Phase 0 — Current monolith]\n    P1[Phase 1 — Backend foundation]\n    P2[Phase 2 — BFF client layer]\n    P3[Phase 3 — UI migration]\n    P4[Phase 4 — Monolith retirement]\n    P5[Phase 5 — Production hardening]\n    P6[Phase 6 — Future optional]\n\n    P0 --\u003e P1 --\u003e P2 --\u003e P3 --\u003e P4 --\u003e P5 --\u003e P6\n```\n\nEach phase below has a **goal**, **scope**, **exit criteria**, and **deliverables**. Phases are designed to be incrementally deployable where possible.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 0 — Current state (baseline)\n\n**Status:** Done (today)\n\n**Architecture:**\n\n```\nWeb (Blazor Server + Handlers + Endpoints + Infrastructure DI)\n  → Domain ← Infrastructure → PostgreSQL\n```\n\n**Characteristics:**\n\n- Two entry points in one process: Blazor `@inject Handler` and Minimal API endpoints\n- Demo auth via scoped [`ICurrentOfficer`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Auth/ICurrentOfficer.cs)\n- Integration tests hit HTTP endpoints on the Web host\n- ~18 Razor files inject handlers directly\n\n**Known pain points this roadmap addresses:**\n\n- Shared `DbContext` per Blazor circuit\n- UI host owns persistence initialization and business logic\n- No true backend deployment boundary\n\n---\n\n## Phase 1 — Backend foundation\n\n**Goal:** Extract the backend into its own deployable host with a proper Application layer, while the monolith can still run unchanged for UI.\n\n**Scope:**\n\n- Create **`SchaerbeekMunicipality.Application`** — move handlers, DTOs, validators, authorization/guards, and auth abstractions from [`Web/Features/`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/)\n- Create **`SchaerbeekMunicipality.Api`** — move `*Endpoint.cs` files and endpoint route groups; wire Infrastructure + Application; move DB initialization from Web\n- Update **[`AppHost.cs`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.AppHost/AppHost.cs)** — orchestrate `postgres` + `api` + `web` with Aspire service discovery\n- Add Api middleware: demo officer resolution, consistent HTTP error mapping (validation → 400, unauthorized → 403, not found → 404, domain conflicts → 409)\n\n**Deliberately not in scope:**\n\n- Changing Domain or Infrastructure internals\n- Migrating Blazor pages to HTTP yet\n- Real authentication\n\n**Exit criteria:**\n\n- Api runs standalone via Aspire and serves all existing `/api/*` routes\n- OpenAPI and health endpoints work on Api\n- Integration tests can target `Api.Program` (may start in this phase or Phase 4)\n- Web can still run as today (dual-host period is acceptable)\n\n**Outcome diagram:**\n\n```\nWeb (unchanged UI, still in-process handlers)     Api (new)\n  → Domain ← Infrastructure ← PostgreSQL            → Application → Domain ← Infrastructure\n```\n\n---\n\n## Phase 2 — BFF client layer\n\n**Goal:** Establish the HTTP boundary between Web and Api before migrating UI pages.\n\n**Scope:**\n\n- Add typed HTTP clients in Web under `Web/Api/`:\n  - `IRegistrationApi` → `/api/registration/*`\n  - `IBirthDeclarationApi` → `/api/birth-declarations/*`\n  - `IChangeOfAddressApi` → `/api/change-of-address/*`\n- Reuse **Application DTOs** as client contracts (no duplicate type definitions)\n- Add `OfficerForwardingHandler` (DelegatingHandler) on all API HttpClients\n- Add `ApiException` helper to map HTTP `ValidationProblemDetails` back to familiar validation errors for existing Blazor catch blocks\n- Register HttpClients with Aspire service discovery URL (`https+http://api`)\n\n**Deliberately not in scope:**\n\n- Removing in-process handler injection from pages\n- A separate `Contracts` project (optional future optimization only)\n\n**Exit criteria:**\n\n- All three API client interfaces implemented and registered\n- At least one end-to-end proof: a client method calls Api successfully with demo officer headers\n- Document upload/download paths validated over HTTP (multipart + streaming)\n\n**Outcome:**\n\nWeb has the plumbing to call the backend; pages still use handlers until Phase 3.\n\n---\n\n## Phase 3 — UI migration (strangler pattern)\n\n**Goal:** Move Blazor UI from in-process handlers to HTTP clients, one feature area at a time.\n\n**Scope (in order):**\n\n1. **Registration** — largest surface: list pages, [`RegistrationCaseDetail.razor`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/Pages/RegistrationCaseDetail.razor), wizard steps, dialogs (~13 handler injections on detail page alone)\n2. **BirthDeclaration** — case list, detail, decision section, parent linking\n3. **ChangeOfAddress** — case list, detail, household moves\n\n**Per-feature migration pattern:**\n\n- Replace `@inject *Handler` with `@inject I*Api`\n- Replace `handler.Handle(request)` with `api.MethodAsync(...)`\n- Keep UI components, DesignSystem, and Municipal components unchanged\n- DTOs flow from Application through HTTP — UI still displays Domain enums via DTO fields\n\n**Benefits unlocked:**\n\n- Each API request gets its own server-side `DbContext` scope\n- Blazor EF concurrency constraints largely disappear\n- Independent reads can use `Task.WhenAll` again (with normal HTTP latency tradeoffs)\n\n**Exit criteria (per feature):**\n\n- All pages/components in the feature area use HTTP clients exclusively\n- No remaining `@inject *Handler` in that feature's Razor files\n- Feature workflows manually verified through Aspire (both hosts running)\n\n**Exit criteria (phase complete):**\n\n- All ~18 handler-injecting Razor files migrated\n- No Blazor page calls Application handlers directly\n\n---\n\n## Phase 4 — Monolith retirement\n\n**Goal:** Web becomes a pure presentation host — no backend concerns remain.\n\n**Scope:**\n\n- Remove from Web [`Program.cs`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Program.cs):\n  - All handler DI registrations (~60 `AddScoped\u003c...Handler\u003e()`)\n  - `AddInfrastructure(...)` and database initialization\n  - Endpoint mapping (`MapRegistrationEndpoints`, etc.)\n- Remove moved code from Web (handlers, endpoints, validators now in Application/Api)\n- Retarget [`Integration.Tests`](tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests) to `Api.Program`; set demo officer headers on test `HttpClient`\n- Update Web `.csproj`: reference Application + ServiceDefaults only; **drop Infrastructure reference**\n\n**Exit criteria:**\n\n- Web project builds and runs with zero Infrastructure dependency\n- All integration tests pass against Api host\n- Domain.Tests unchanged and passing\n- Single clear runtime path: **Web → HTTP → Api → Application → Domain/Infrastructure**\n\n**Final architecture:**\n\n```\nAppHost\n  ├── PostgreSQL\n  ├── Api (Application + Infrastructure + endpoints)\n  └── Web (Blazor + HttpClients → Api)\n         references Application (DTOs only, no Infrastructure)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Phase 5 — Production hardening\n\n**Goal:** Replace demo auth and address operational concerns for a real municipal deployment.\n\n**Scope:**\n\n- **Real authentication:** OpenID Connect / Entra ID (or municipal IdP) on Api; JWT bearer or secure cookies\n- Replace `DemoOfficerMiddleware` with claims-based `ICurrentOfficer`\n- Replace `OfficerForwardingHandler` with token acquisition in Web BFF\n- **Document storage:** Evaluate [`LocalFileDocumentStorage`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Infrastructure/Storage/LocalFileDocumentStorage.cs) for production; swap `IDocumentStorage` to blob storage if Api needs horizontal scaling (Application handlers unchanged)\n- **Observability:** Ensure distributed tracing spans Web → Api (Aspire/OpenTelemetry already scaffolded in ServiceDefaults)\n- **Security review:** Header trust boundaries, health endpoint protection (already partially in ServiceDefaults)\n\n**Exit criteria:**\n\n- No demo officer headers in production path\n- Auth enforced at Api for all endpoints\n- Document storage strategy documented and implemented for target environment\n\n---\n\n## Phase 6 — Future optional (not scheduled)\n\nThese are **not** part of the near-term roadmap. Pursue only when a concrete need appears:\n\n| Option | Trigger |\n|--------|---------|\n| **Contracts project** | Web must not reference Application (separate repo, NuGet-published SDK, WASM client) |\n| **Blazor WebAssembly** | True SPA requirement, offline needs |\n| **Additional API hosts** | Different security zones (public citizen portal vs internal staff API) |\n| **Message bus / async integrations** | Real outbound integrations to tax office, social security, national register |\n| **MediatR or source-generated DI** | Handler count becomes painful to register manually |\n| **Playwright E2E tests** | Full-stack UI regression against Aspire |\n\n---\n\n## Dependency and risk summary\n\n| Risk | Mitigation | Phase |\n|------|------------|-------|\n| Large file moves break git history | Move whole feature folders per PR | 1, 3 |\n| HTTP error handling differs from in-process exceptions | `ApiException` maps ProblemDetails to familiar types | 2 |\n| Document upload/download over HTTP | Pilot in Registration first | 2, 3 |\n| Added latency vs in-process | Acceptable for back-office; existing loading states help | 3 |\n| Dual-host complexity during migration | Aspire orchestration; feature-by-feature strangler | 1–3 |\n| Over-engineering | One Api, no microservices, no MediatR unless needed | All |\n\n---\n\n## Rough effort guidance (for planning, not scheduling)\n\n| Phase | Relative effort |\n|-------|-----------------|\n| Phase 1 — Backend foundation | Small–medium (mechanical extraction) |\n| Phase 2 — BFF client layer | Small |\n| Phase 3 — UI migration | Medium–large (18 Razor files, 3 feature areas) |\n| Phase 4 — Monolith retirement | Small |\n| Phase 5 — Production hardening | Medium (depends on IdP choice) |\n| Phase 6 — Future optional | Undefined |\n\nPhases 1–4 together represent a **clean architectural split** with demo auth preserved. Phase 5 is when it becomes **production-ready**.\n",

_**Agent (default)**_

If you say **"implement this plan"**, I would **write code** — not just documentation.

The roadmap we created is the **guide**. Implementing it means doing the actual engineering work:

| Phase | What gets built (code, not docs) |
|-------|----------------------------------|
| **Phase 1** | New `Application` and `Api` projects, move handlers/DTOs/endpoints, update `AppHost`, wire DI and middleware |
| **Phase 2** | Typed `HttpClient` classes in Web, `OfficerForwardingHandler`, error mapping |
| **Phase 3** | Change Razor files from `@inject Handler` to `@inject IRegistrationApi` etc. |
| **Phase 4** | Strip Infrastructure/handlers/endpoints from Web, retarget integration tests |
| **Phase 5** | Real auth, storage changes — when you're ready for that |

So: **documentation = the roadmap** (already done). **Implementation = refactor the solution** across multiple projects and files.

A few practical notes:

1. **Scope matters.** "Implement the plan" could mean all of Phases 1–4, or just "start Phase 1." I'd typically begin with Phase 1 and confirm before moving on, since it's a large change.

2. **You'd need Agent mode.** Right now I'm in Ask mode — I can only explain. Switch to Agent mode and say something like *"Implement Phase 1 of the backend split roadmap"* to have me actually make the changes.

3. **I won't commit unless you ask.** Per your rules, I'd make the code changes but only create a git commit if you explicitly request it.

If you want to proceed, a good first prompt would be: **"Implement Phase 1 of the backend split roadmap."** That keeps the first chunk reviewable before touching all the Blazor pages.

Evaluating lightweight .NET messaging options. The existing IDomainEventDispatcher provides a foundation. In-process outbox patterns and EF Core outbox with background processors are the lightest candidates. External buses like MassTransit or RabbitMQ would be heavier.

The recommendation will build on the existing DomainEventDispatcher rather than introducing heavy external messaging systems. Reviewing the current domain event infrastructure to make the plan concrete.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Existing domain events such as RegistrationConfirmed, BirthRegistered, and AddressChanged provide a foundation for the async integration.

Phase 5 will use a lightweight transactional outbox pattern. Integration messages will be stored in the database alongside domain changes and processed by a background service. This avoids new infrastructure. A .NET Channels approach with a hosted worker is also possible. Outbound notifications already exist in the database and UI. Playwright tests will be added to this phase.

A dedicated E2E test project will run against the Aspire stack. It will cover key flows such as opening a case, registration workflows, and birth declarations. Demo officer query parameters or a role switcher will support testing. The plan will be restructured into five phases, removing production hardening and deferring authentication items to a future section.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Existing BffApiClientTests provide useful context for the revised plan.

Phases 0 through 4 are complete. Phase 5 now covers two tracks: a lightweight async integration using a Transactional Outbox pattern, and Playwright end-to-end tests. The outbox pattern enqueues messages atomically alongside domain commits.

A BackgroundService worker in the API will poll the outbox and dispatch messages to `IIntegrationHandler` implementations. Each recipient gets its own `IOutboundIntegration` interface. Simulated HTTP clients will handle outbound calls for now. Aspire RabbitMQ is reserved as an optional future upgrade.

The existing OutboundNotification table provides the foundation for the outbox pattern. Phase 5 will add a processing pipeline where a background worker picks up pending notifications and invokes integration adapters. Retry logic and status tracking for Pending, Sent, and Failed states will be included. Domain events will publish to the outbox instead of dispatching synchronously. This keeps the architecture lightweight without requiring RabbitMQ.

Playwright end-to-end tests will live in `tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.E2E.Tests` using `Microsoft.Playwright`. Fixtures will target either Aspire or a web URL from environment variables. The suite will cover key user journeys and integrate into CI. Real authentication is deferred to a later phase rather than Phase 5. Phase 6 will be reduced to a minimal deferred list without message bus or Playwright work.
</details></think>

{
  "name": "Backend Split Roadmap",
  "overview": "Updated backend split roadmap: Phases 0–4 complete the architectural split with demo auth preserved. Phase 5 (new) adds lightweight async outbound integrations via a transactional outbox and Playwright E2E tests against the full Aspire stack. Real auth and production hardening are explicitly deferred.",
  "plan": "# Backend split roadmap (updated)\n\n## Status summary\n\n| Phase | Name | Status |\n|-------|------|--------|\n| 0 | Current monolith (baseline) | Done |\n| 1 | Backend foundation (Application + Api) | Done |\n| 2 | BFF client layer (typed HttpClients) | Done |\n| 3 | UI migration (strangler per feature) | Done |\n| 4 | Monolith retirement (Web = presentation only) | Done |\n| **5** | **Async integrations + Playwright E2E** | **Next** |\n\nPhases 1–4 delivered the target split:\n\n```\nAppHost\n  ├── PostgreSQL\n  ├── Api (Application + Infrastructure + endpoints)\n  └── Web (Blazor BFF + HttpClients → Api)\n```\n\nDemo officer auth via forwarded headers remains in place — **real auth is not on this roadmap**.\n\n---\n\n## Architectural principles (unchanged)\n\n- **Application** is the backend; **Api** is a thin HTTP adapter; **Web** has no Infrastructure reference\n- **One Api project** — no microservices\n- **Vertical slices** preserved in Application\n- **Web → business data** always via HTTP → Api → Application at runtime\n- **Web** may transitively reference Domain enums through Application DTOs (`DocumentType`, `RegistrationCaseStatus`, etc.)\n\nSee prior phases 0–4 in the full roadmap for layer diagrams and migration details.\n\n---\n\n## Phase overview (revised)\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n    P0[Phase 0 — Baseline]\n    P1[Phase 1 — Backend foundation]\n    P2[Phase 2 — BFF clients]\n    P3[Phase 3 — UI migration]\n    P4[Phase 4 — Monolith retirement]\n    P5[Phase 5 — Async integrations and E2E]\n\n    P0 --\u003e P1 --\u003e P2 --\u003e P3 --\u003e P4 --\u003e P5\n```\n\n---\n\n## Phase 5 — Async integrations and Playwright E2E\n\n**Goal:** Evolve simulated outbound notifications into a lightweight, reliable async integration pipeline — and add full-stack browser tests to guard critical officer workflows.\n\nThis phase has **two parallel workstreams** that can proceed independently:\n\n```mermaid\nflowchart TB\n    subgraph workstreamA [Workstream A — Lightweight async]\n        DomainEvent[Domain events]\n        Outbox[Integration outbox table]\n        Worker[Background worker in Api]\n        Adapters[Integration adapters]\n        External[External systems — simulated first]\n    end\n\n    subgraph workstreamB [Workstream B — Playwright E2E]\n        Aspire[Aspire full stack]\n        Playwright[Playwright test project]\n        Journeys[Critical user journeys]\n        CI[CI pipeline hook]\n    end\n\n    DomainEvent --\u003e Outbox\n    Outbox --\u003e Worker\n    Worker --\u003e Adapters\n    Adapters --\u003e External\n\n    Aspire --\u003e Playwright\n    Playwright --\u003e Journeys\n    Journeys --\u003e CI\n```\n\n---\n\n### Workstream A — Lightweight async integrations\n\n#### Starting point (already in codebase)\n\nToday, domain events are dispatched **synchronously in-process** via [`DomainEventDispatcher`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Infrastructure/Events/DomainEventDispatcher.cs). Handlers like [`RegistrationConfirmedNotificationHandler`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Infrastructure/Events/RegistrationConfirmedNotificationHandler.cs) immediately write [`OutboundNotification`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Domain/Notifications/) records to PostgreSQL — simulating messages to tax administration, social security, and health insurance.\n\nThis is a good foundation. Phase 5 makes delivery **async, retryable, and adapter-based** without introducing a heavy message broker.\n\n#### Recommended approach: transactional outbox (not RabbitMQ)\n\n**Why outbox over a message bus:**\n\n| Approach | Fit |\n|----------|-----|\n| **Transactional outbox + BackgroundService** | Lightweight, no new infrastructure, works with existing PostgreSQL and Aspire, survives restarts |\n| RabbitMQ / Azure Service Bus / MassTransit | Overkill until Api runs multiple instances or external systems require pub/sub |\n\n**Target flow:**\n\n```mermaid\nsequenceDiagram\n    participant Handler as ConfirmRegistrationHandler\n    participant DB as PostgreSQL\n    participant Worker as IntegrationOutboxWorker\n    participant Adapter as TaxAdministrationAdapter\n    participant Admin as OutboundNotificationsPage\n\n    Handler-\u003e\u003eDB: Save domain changes + enqueue outbox message atomically\n    Handler--\u003e\u003eHandler: Return HTTP response immediately\n\n    loop Poll every N seconds\n        Worker-\u003e\u003eDB: Fetch pending outbox messages\n        Worker-\u003e\u003eAdapter: Deliver message\n        Adapter--\u003e\u003eWorker: Success or failure\n        Worker-\u003e\u003eDB: Mark Sent or schedule retry\n    end\n\n    Admin-\u003e\u003eDB: List outbound notifications status\n```\n\n#### Scope\n\n**Domain / Infrastructure:**\n\n- Extend `OutboundNotification` (or add `IntegrationOutboxMessage`) with delivery lifecycle: `Pending` → `Processing` → `Sent` / `Failed`, plus `AttemptCount`, `NextAttemptAt`, `LastError`\n- Refactor domain-event handlers to **enqueue outbox messages** instead of calling external systems inline (keep the same business triggers: `RegistrationConfirmed`, `BirthRegistered`, `AddressChanged`)\n- Add `IIntegrationAdapter` interface per recipient (`TaxAdministration`, `SocialSecurity`, `HealthInsurance`, etc.) with **simulated implementations** first (log + delay + mark sent)\n- Add `IntegrationOutboxWorker` (`BackgroundService` in Api) — polls pending messages, dispatches to adapters, handles retries with exponential backoff\n- Ensure outbox writes participate in the same EF transaction as domain saves (same `SaveChangesAsync` boundary)\n\n**Application / Api:**\n\n- Optional: endpoint to manually retry failed notifications (administration page already lists them via [`ListOutboundNotifications`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Application/Features/Registration/ListOutboundNotifications/))\n- Domain event dispatch becomes: publish to outbox → worker delivers asynchronously (handlers return faster)\n\n**Deliberately not in scope:**\n\n- External message broker (RabbitMQ, Kafka, Service Bus)\n- MediatR or other command buses\n- Real HTTP integrations to government systems (adapters stay simulated; swap implementations later without changing outbox/worker)\n\n#### Upgrade path (when needed, not Phase 5)\n\nIf Api scales to multiple instances, replace the polling worker with **Aspire-managed RabbitMQ** or **PostgreSQL `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`** for competing consumers — the outbox table and adapter interfaces stay the same.\n\n#### Exit criteria (Workstream A)\n\n- Confirming registration returns before all outbound deliveries complete\n- Outbox messages persist and survive Api restart\n- Failed deliveries retry with backoff; permanently failed messages visible in admin UI\n- At least one integration test covers enqueue → worker → `Sent` lifecycle\n- Existing [`CertificatesAndNotificationsTests`](tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests/Features/Registration/CertificatesAndNotificationsTests.cs) updated or supplemented for async behavior\n\n---\n\n### Workstream B — Playwright E2E tests\n\n#### Why now\n\nIntegration tests ([`SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests`](tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests/)) cover the **API layer** (including [`BffApiClientTests`](tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Integration.Tests/Api/BffApiClientTests.cs)). After Phases 1–4, nothing systematically verifies **Blazor UI flows** through Web → Api → DB end-to-end.\n\n#### Scope\n\n**New test project:** `tests/SchaerbeekMunicipality.E2E.Tests/`\n\n- **Microsoft.Playwright** for browser automation\n- Test fixture that targets the **Web** URL (Aspire-assigned or configurable via `E2E_BASE_URL`)\n- Demo officer setup via existing [`DemoOfficerPersistence`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Auth/DemoOfficerPersistence.cs) query param or RoleSwitcher interaction\n\n**Initial journey coverage (priority order):**\n\n1. **Registration intake** — open case, record identity, declare address\n2. **Registration decision** — population officer confirms case; verify status chip updates\n3. **Birth declaration** — open case, link parent, confirm\n4. **Change of address** — open case, declare new address\n5. **Outbound notifications admin** — verify notifications appear after confirmation (ties to Workstream A)\n\n**Patterns:**\n\n- Page Object Model for shared components (`AppPage`, dialogs, role switcher)\n- Stable selectors: prefer `data-testid` attributes on key interactive elements (add minimally where MudBlazor renders ambiguous markup)\n- Run against Aspire stack locally; CI runs `dotnet run AppHost` + Playwright in pipeline\n\n**Deliberately not in scope:**\n\n- Visual regression / screenshot comparison\n- Load testing\n- Testing every wizard step exhaustively (start with happy paths + one role-boundary case)\n\n#### Exit criteria (Workstream B)\n\n- E2E project builds and runs locally against Aspire\n- At least **3 critical journeys** automated (registration open + confirm, birth declaration, change of address)\n- One role-boundary test (e.g. reception officer cannot confirm registration)\n- Documented `dotnet test` command for E2E suite\n- CI job runs E2E on PR or nightly (team choice)\n\n---\n\n## Explicitly deferred (not on this roadmap)\n\nThese were previously Phase 5/6 items — **out of scope unless a new roadmap is created:**\n\n| Item | Notes |\n|------|-------|\n| **Real authentication (OIDC/JWT/Entra ID)** | Demo officer headers remain; `ICurrentOfficer` abstraction makes future swap possible |\n| **Blob document storage** | [`LocalFileDocumentStorage`](src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Infrastructure/Storage/LocalFileDocumentStorage.cs) stays on Api filesystem |\n| **Contracts project** | Web referencing Application for DTOs is sufficient |\n| **Blazor WebAssembly** | Staying on Blazor Server BFF |\n| **Additional API hosts** | One Api project |\n| **MediatR / source-generated DI** | Manual handler registration is fine |\n\n---\n\n## Phase 5 risks and mitigations\n\n| Risk | Mitigation |\n|------|------------|\n| Outbox worker races with multiple Api instances | Use `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` or single-instance worker until scaling needed |\n| Flaky Playwright tests (timing, MudBlazor overlays) | Explicit waits on network/selector; `data-testid` on critical buttons |\n| E2E suite slow in CI | Run subset on PR, full suite nightly; parallelize journeys |\n| Async delivery breaks existing notification tests | Update integration tests to await worker or trigger processing explicitly in test harness |\n| Over-engineering the message layer | Outbox + BackgroundService only; no broker in Phase 5 |\n\n---\n\n## Rough effort guidance\n\n| Workstream | Relative effort |\n|------------|-----------------|\n| A — Outbox + worker + adapters | Medium |\n| B — Playwright project + 3–5 journeys | Medium |\n| **Phase 5 total** | **Medium–large** (can split across two PRs) |\n\nPhases 0–4 = **architectural split complete**. Phase 5 = **operational maturity**: reliable async outbound delivery and UI regression safety net.\n",

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Aspire.Hosting.Testing will be used for the E2E setup.

Aspire.Hosting.Testing version 13.0.2 will be added alongside Microsoft.Playwright. An E2EAppFixture implementing IAsyncLifetime is being drafted to manage the distributed application lifecycle.

The Aspire testing API was reviewed, but a simpler WebApplicationFactory approach is preferred for reliability.

E2E requires both the Web and Api services. Aspire.Hosting.Testing would need Docker for Postgres in CI. Two WebApplicationFactories are being considered instead. The Web factory would target the Api at a test server URL. Service discovery addresses would need overriding.

Reviewing AddMunicipalApiClients to understand how API clients are configured.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The web project needs to expose its Program class for testing. I'll update Web.csproj to include it.

Adding `InternalsVisibleTo` to Web.csproj exposes internal types to the E2E test assembly. A public partial `Program` class will be added to Web.csproj so MVC Testing can discover it. An E2E fixture combining both API and Web app factories is being defined.

The E2E fixture initializes both factories during setup and disposes them afterward. The web factory wraps `Web.Program` and receives the API factory as a dependency.

The web factory constructor stores the API factory instance. During host configuration, it sets the environment to Development and reconfigures the registration API client to target the API factory's server address.

Replacing the API client's HttpClient registration is tricky because AddMunicipalApiClients already registered it. Removing IHttpClientFactory registrations through ConfigureTestServices is difficult. A configuration override looks like a better approach.

Overriding the base address via `UseSetting` would require modifying `AddMunicipalApiClients` to read from configuration. A cleaner alternative is to use `builder.ConfigureServices` to remove the existing HTTP client registrations and reconfigure the API clients afterward. Other patterns like `PostConfigureAll` or `IConfigureOptions<HttpClientFactoryOptions>` are also being considered.

The simplest fix is to add an `AddMunicipalApiClientsForTesting` extension method or use an environment variable for the test base URL. The preferred approach removes the existing typed API client registrations inside `ConfigureWebHost` and replaces them with test versions.

Removing typed API client registrations from the DI container is too complex because typed HTTP clients register multiple descriptors. The simpler path is to let DependencyInjection read the test base address from IConfiguration instead.

The `AddMunicipalApiClients` extension method will accept `IConfiguration` as a parameter. It will read the API base address from `MunicipalApi:BaseAddress` when available, otherwise fall back to the existing development or production default.

Overriding the base address via configuration alone is insufficient. The web application's HttpClient must also use the API factory's in-memory test handler to avoid network traffic. This means the E2E factory still needs to override the primary HTTP message handler. The approach is shifting toward using `ConfigureTestServices` within `ConfigureWebHost` to replace the existing API client registrations.

The E2E factory will register `IRegistrationApi` directly as a scoped singleton. It will instantiate `RegistrationApiClient` manually using the API factory's in-memory handler and base address. This bypasses the typed HTTP client registration entirely. The next step is to outline Playwright tests.

Playwright tests will run under a collection fixture named `MunicipalE2EFixture`. Each test will receive a browser page and navigate to the web factory server address, passing a demo officer query parameter. The demo officer persistence logic exists in the Application layer, but its exact integration point in the Web project—likely `MainLayout`—still needs verification.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
A hybrid approach will handle complex end-to-end test flows.

API seeding via HttpClient will handle lengthy UI flows. Playwright will verify key pages. RegistrationIntakeE2ETest will cover case opening through the UI. RegistrationConfirmE2ETest will seed a case via API, then navigate to the detail page in the browser to verify the decision section and confirm.

BirthDeclarationE2ETest will open a birth case via the UI. ChangeOfAddressE2ETest will search for a NR and open a case. RoleBoundaryE2ETest will verify access restrictions on the cases page. The test fixture will expose ApiFactory.Services for seeding before Playwright navigation. Registration confirmation will use the CreateCaseReadyForDecisionAsync pattern.

Infrastructure comes first. The web application will become a partial class with InternalsVisibleTo for end-to-end tests. The test factory will configure an in-memory API handler. HttpClient registrations will be replaced rather than removed.

The replacement logic will live entirely in the E2E test project's `MunicipalWebAppFactory`. A duplicate `AddHttpClient` registration there will override the production API clients. This avoids touching the Web project directly.

The factory will remove the production API client registrations by filtering the service collection for types such as `IRegistrationApi` and `RegistrationApiClient`. This replaces the earlier plan to override registrations with duplicate entries.

The factory will inject a custom `HttpMessageHandler` and a test connection helper into the service collection. It will also register `IRegistrationApi` as a scoped delegate that resolves the handler from the container.

The scoped delegate will create a new HttpClient with the test handler and base address, then wrap it in a RegistrationApiClient. However, this risks a conflict because Program.cs already registers IRegistrationApi via AddHttpClient, and a later scoped registration may clash.

The cleanest fix is to add an internal extension in the Web project that configures the primary API handler. This avoids the DI conflicts caused by overriding registrations in the test factory.

The internal extension will set the API client base address and wire the custom message handler. Removing existing HTTP client registrations in the factory is too complex. The simpler path is to use `WithWebHostBuilder` to replace services after the application builds.

BFF client tests build a separate ServiceCollection, but end-to-end tests require a full Web host. Using an IStartupFilter to swap registrations was rejected as too hacky. The chosen path is to extend the Web project's DependencyInjection directly, adding an overload to AddMunicipalApiClients that accepts a custom HttpMessageHandler and base address.

The Web project's dependency injection will read `Testing:ApiBaseAddress` from configuration to set the API client base address. A new `Testing:ApiIntegration:Enabled` flag will gate the switch. A custom `DelegatingHandler` will forward requests to the test server instead of the production API.

The `ConfigurePrimaryHttpMessageHandler` must be set during `AddHttpClient`, so `AddMunicipalApiClients` will now accept an optional `IConfiguration` parameter. A `Testing:UseInProcessApi` flag will toggle between the in-process test handler and the production API clients.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Creating end-to-end infrastructure files.

Defining the E2E collection fixture and async lifetime setup. Adding UI test helpers, page objects, and data-testid attributes.

The fixture initializes both factories and launches a headless Chromium browser via Playwright.

The web app factory configures the Development environment and injects a singleton API bridge that connects to the existing API server.

Realized the API bridge must be registered before `AddMunicipalApiClients` runs in `Program.cs`. Otherwise the client registration sees no bridge and fails. The fix is to register the bridge inside `ConfigureWebHost` before the application builds.

The bridge registration inside `ConfigureWebHost` still happens too late. `AddMunicipalApiClients` runs during host building before `ConfigureWebHost` callbacks execute. Evaluating four fixes: moving the bridge check to `IConfigureOptions`, using `builder.UseSetting` with early host configuration, reading configuration in `AddMunicipalApiClients`, or ordering `WebHostBuilderCallback`.

Confirmed that `ConfigureWebHost` callbacks run after `Program.cs` top-level statements. This means the bridge is registered too late for `AddMunicipalApiClients` to detect it. `builder.ConfigureTestServices` would face the same ordering problem.

Evaluated three fixes. Using `builder.UseSetting` to defer client registration was considered. Using a `HostingStartup` extension was rejected as impractical from the factory. Changing `AddMunicipalApiClients` to always register and swap the HTTP handler later was also considered. The preferred approach is to register the bridge in `ConfigureWebHost` and then re-run or replace the client registrations so the bridge is available at resolution time.

Decided against replacing existing HTTP client registrations in `ConfigureWebHost`; that approach is too messy. Instead, `AddMunicipalApiClients` will be refactored to use a factory or delegate pattern. A private `RegisterClient` helper will handle per-client registration, allowing the API bridge to be injected at resolution time rather than at service registration time.

The `RegisterClient` helper now uses `AddHttpClient` with a delegate. It resolves the API bridge at resolution time. If the bridge exists, it sets the client base address and returns. Otherwise it falls back to the development or production API URL. The primary HTTP message handler is also configured through the same delegate, swapping to the bridge's custom handler when available.

The bridge is resolved at HttpClient build time, not at registration time. It can therefore be registered in ConfigureWebHost after AddMunicipalApiClients, provided it is present before the first HTTP call. DependencyInjection.cs will be updated with this approach.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The reception officer role boundary test redirects from /registration/cases to /registration/new-case via RegistrationRoleGate.

Internal integration test helpers cannot be used directly in E2E tests without InternalsVisibleTo. The plan is to add that attribute to Integration.Tests.csproj so E2E can access the existing seed logic.

The E2E seeding strategy is now to inject public Application handlers directly via ApiFactory.Services rather than duplicating seed logic. A minimal registration case seeder may still be added to E2E/Seeding/RegistrationCaseSeeder.cs.

The InternalsVisibleTo attribute is being added to Integration.Tests.csproj. Existing RegistrationTestHelpers and CaseDecisionTests patterns will be reused in E2E.

The change of address E2E flow is being mapped out for population officers. It covers searching the national register for a citizen and verifying navigation to the case detail page.

The birth declaration E2E flow for the reception officer is also being outlined.

MudSelect dropdowns in the birth declaration flow will be handled by adding data-testid attributes to the UI components. The E2E bootstrap will install Playwright with dependencies, and the CI pipeline will include an E2E job for this purpose.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Creating the E2E seeding helper and test files.

The E2E seeding helper will use integration API helpers. Registration confirmation requires approval first. The flow seeds a ready case, approves it via API, navigates to the detail page, clicks confirm, and verifies the registered status. A separate UI-only test covers registration intake.

Birth declaration E2E will seed a ready case and verify "Birth registered" after confirmation. Change of address E2E will search for Jean Dupont and confirm the case detail page loads. Role boundary E2E will verify reception redirects to /registration/new-case. A registration status test will seed an approved case and confirm in the UI. The approve step may be seeded via API rather than through UI.

RegiseSeeder will use internal integration test helpers. E2E tests will carry a Category trait to support CI filtering. The CI workflow needs a Playwright installation step. Tests and a README will be created.

MudBlazor popover selection will handle the birth declaration visit reason on the new case page. The option name must match the localized label text.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The Program type is in the global namespace, so the WebApplicationFactory fully qualified name needs adjustment.

A public partial class named WebProgram will be added to the Web project. Program.cs will be renamed to WebProgram. This resolves the global namespace conflict for WebApplicationFactory.

The E2E project will drop its direct references to Api and Infrastructure. Integration.Tests will provide the MunicipalAppFactory instead. The E2E csproj will only reference Web, Integration.Tests, and Application.

The global::Program alias will not compile because Api also defines a Program class in the global namespace. The WebApplicationFactory will instead use `WebApplicationFactory<Program>` with a `using SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web;` directive.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
WebFactory.Server.BaseAddress is set to `http://localhost` without a port. TestServer may not listen on a real TCP socket for Playwright.

Playwright requires a real HTTP server rather than an in-memory TestServer. The fix is to use WebApplicationFactory with a real Kestrel listener on a random port.

The concrete implementation uses `builder.UseUrls("http://127.0.0.1:0")` to bind Kestrel to a random local port. This lets Playwright connect via a real TCP socket instead of the in-memory TestServer.

The concrete implementation now sets `WebHostDefaults.ServerUrlsKey` to `http://127.0.0.1:0` inside `ConfigureWebHost`. The test client is created with `WebApplicationFactoryClientOptions` that disables auto-redirects and explicitly targets that base address. A more robust alternative is to retrieve the actual bound address from the `IServer` service rather than hardcoding the URL.

The concrete implementation now uses `builder.ConfigureKestrel` with `Listen(IPAddress.Loopback, 0)` to bind Kestrel to a random local port. The actual bound address is retrieved from `Server.Features.Get<IServer>().Addresses.First()` and exposed through a `ServerAddress` property on the factory. This replaces the earlier `UseUrls` approach and avoids hardcoding the URL.

The `CreateHost` override now extracts the actual bound address from `IServerAddressesFeature` and stores it in `ServerAddress`. `MunicipalWebAppFactory` will be updated to use this Kestrel-based approach.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The proxy approach seems overly complex and likely incorrect.

WebApplicationFactory defaults to TestServer. Using Kestrel directly instead should resolve the issue.

Overriding `CreateHost` to build and start the host directly avoids the TestServer wrapper. The factory can then retrieve the actual server address from `IServer`.

The factory extracts the real server URL from `IServerAddressesFeature`. It forces Kestrel via `UseKestrel()` and binds to `http://127.0.0.1:0` so the address is known before startup.

MunicipalWebAppFactory can be simplified significantly by replacing the full host creation logic.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
CreateClient() may be initializing before the host binds, leaving the address as 127.0.0.1:0.

The fix is to remove CreateClient() and instead start the host directly inside InitializeAsync. A new StartHost method on WebFactory will build and launch the host, then set BaseUri after the port is assigned.

IServerAddressesFeature likely returns the configured URL template before the host actually binds. A polling loop could wait for real addresses, or a TcpListener could reserve a free port beforehand.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The connection is still refused on the valid port.

WebApplicationFactory likely creates two separate hosts, causing the client to connect to a disposed TestServer. I will try a different approach by starting the API factory directly and using Host.CreateDefaultBuilder instead.

Microsoft's documentation confirms that Playwright cannot target the TestServer directly. The best path forward is to run the Web host as a real process using `WebApplication.CreateBuilder()`, replicating the configuration from `Program.cs`.

Duplicating Program.cs setup is undesirable. Extracting `AddWebPresentation` would let me reuse the existing configuration. Alternatively, I can follow the Microsoft ASP.NET Core repository pattern: skip the base `CreateHost` call that generates the TestServer host, then build a real host with `Host.CreateDefaultBuilder`.

The override pattern is flawed because `CreateDefaultClient` spawns a separate TestServer host through base-class delegation. The custom `CreateHost` Kestrel host may be disposed or ignored entirely. I need to inspect the `WebApplicationFactory` source to understand its internal host lifecycle.

The connection refusal likely stems from `CreateDefaultClient` invoking the base `CreateHost`, which spawns a TestServer wrapper that stops the custom Kestrel host. I will abandon overriding `CreateHost` and instead add an `EnsureStarted()` method to manage the host lifecycle directly.

The chosen approach is a dedicated `MunicipalWebHost` class implementing `IAsyncDisposable`. It will spin up a fresh `WebApplication` via `WebApplication.CreateBuilder` with `WebApplicationOptions`, bypassing `WebApplicationFactory` entirely. This avoids the dual-host lifecycle issues that caused the connection refused errors.

The `MunicipalWebHost` implementation now mirrors `Program.cs` exactly. It configures the builder with the development environment, binds to the target port, registers the API bridge, adds service defaults, and loads the web presentation and API clients. The resulting application starts asynchronously and exposes its base URI.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Switching to a direct `WebApplication` host — `WebApplicationFactory` isn't binding Kestrel reliably for Playwright.

The test script waits for `window.Blazor` and a data-testid element. MudForm validation may fail silently in a headless browser. The page might lack `@rendermode InteractiveServer`. The button click may not reach the server component.

All pages use `InteractiveServer`, so the component is interactive. The test should target the enabled button by its data-testid or ARIA role. A screenshot on failure would help diagnose the issue. The UI update may require a SignalR roundtrip that does not complete in the headless browser. The snackbar text "Registration case created." should confirm whether the server response was received.

The test will wait for the MudSnackbar message "Registration case created" rather than relying on the button click alone. The API bridge is confirmed working since the logs showed HTTP 200. Both the Web host and the test server share the same in-memory SQLite database through the same factory. The test should also check for alternative success indicators such as "Case created" or "The intake file is open". The interactivity wait time will be increased.

The test will wait for the Blazor framework to be defined before clicking the button. It will also wait for the specific data-testid element to appear. The App.razor file needs to be checked to confirm the render mode setting.
